Re: [PATCH v2 13/14] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch adds zero-copy support over io-uring for FUSE, but raises security concerns about direct access to client pages.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about the necessity of CAP_SYS_ADMIN for zero-copy support, prompting an explanation from Joanne Koong.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 2026-04-30 at 13:35 +0100, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Thu, Apr 30, 2026 at 12:42 PM Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:
> > 
> > On Thu, 2026-04-02 at 09:28 -0700, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > > Implement zero-copy data transfer for fuse over io-uring, eliminating
> > > memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for
> > > page-backed read/write operations.
> > > 
> > > When the FUSE_URING_ZERO_COPY flag is set alongside FUSE_URING_BUFRING,
> > > the kernel registers the client's underlying pages as a sparse buffer at
> > > the entry's fixed id via io_buffer_register_bvec(). The fuse server can
> > > then perform io_uring read/write operations directly on these pages.
> > > Non-page-backed args (eg out headers) go through the payload buffer as
> > > normal.
> > > 
> > > This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and buffer rings with pinned headers and
> > > buffers. Gating on pinned headers and buffers keeps the configuration
> > > space small and avoids partially-optimized modes that are unlikely to be
> > > useful in practice. Pages are unregistered when the request completes.
> > > 
> > 
> > Can you elaborate a bit more on why CAP_SYS_ADMIN is needed here? It's
> > not immediately obvious to me.
> 
> Thank you for reviewing this series, Jeff!
> 
> This is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN because zero-copy allows the server
> direct access to the client's underlying pages, rather than operating
> on an intermediary buffer that the contents of client's pages were
> copied into. A malicious unprivileged server could keep direct access
> to the client's pages (eg even if the client tries to cancel a
> read/write, if the request was already sent to userspace, the server
> will still have access to the underlying pages). In the non-zero-copy
> path this isn't possible because the server only operates on the copy
> of the pages and not on the actual pages.
> 

Thanks for the explanation. I'd suggest adding that to the commit
message (and maybe comments near the CAP_SYS_ADMIN checks) in case
others aren't clear why this is gated on that.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Provided an explanation for the CAP_SYS_ADMIN requirement, citing security concerns about direct access to client pages.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Apr 30, 2026 at 12:42 PM Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:
>
> On Thu, 2026-04-02 at 09:28 -0700, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > Implement zero-copy data transfer for fuse over io-uring, eliminating
> > memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for
> > page-backed read/write operations.
> >
> > When the FUSE_URING_ZERO_COPY flag is set alongside FUSE_URING_BUFRING,
> > the kernel registers the client's underlying pages as a sparse buffer at
> > the entry's fixed id via io_buffer_register_bvec(). The fuse server can
> > then perform io_uring read/write operations directly on these pages.
> > Non-page-backed args (eg out headers) go through the payload buffer as
> > normal.
> >
> > This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and buffer rings with pinned headers and
> > buffers. Gating on pinned headers and buffers keeps the configuration
> > space small and avoids partially-optimized modes that are unlikely to be
> > useful in practice. Pages are unregistered when the request completes.
> >
>
> Can you elaborate a bit more on why CAP_SYS_ADMIN is needed here? It's
> not immediately obvious to me.

Thank you for reviewing this series, Jeff!

This is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN because zero-copy allows the server
direct access to the client's underlying pages, rather than operating
on an intermediary buffer that the contents of client's pages were
copied into. A malicious unprivileged server could keep direct access
to the client's pages (eg even if the client tries to cancel a
read/write, if the request was already sent to userspace, the server
will still have access to the underlying pages). In the non-zero-copy
path this isn't possible because the server only operates on the copy
of the pages and not on the actual pages.

Thanks,
Joanne
>
> --
> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 0/7]] VFS: Prepare to lift lookup out of exclusive lock for directory ops
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential lock imbalance issue in the VFS directory operations by ensuring that the parent lock is reacquired before exiting early from certain lookup functions. The problem arises when d_alloc_parallel() or d_alloc() return without holding the lock, causing the caller to unlock an unheld rwsem and corrupting its state. To fix this, the patch modifies the early return paths in these functions to reacquire the parent lock before returning.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: NeilBrown noted that the parent lock is dropped unconditionally before d_alloc_parallel() and three early return paths exit without reacquiring it, leading to a potential corruption of the rwsem state when inode_unlock_shared() is called after ->lookup() returns. He suggested moving the unlock/relock around d_alloc_parallel(), which resolves the issue.
sentiment_signals: reviewer provided a specific solution to address the issue

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: fuse/io-uring: Proposal to support pBuf in additon to kBuf
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes to support pBuf in addition to kBuf in the fuse/io-uring interface, allowing for more efficient handling of small requests and reducing kernel-side complexity. The current implementation using multiple queues for different buffer sizes is seen as suboptimal due to fragmentation and inefficient distribution of buffers. The proposed solution involves separate payload-size queues, which simplifies kernel-side logic and improves performance. This change aims to address issues with head-of-line blocking and non-blocking IO, making it more feasible for fuse servers to use io-uring without requiring significant modifications.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Bernd Schubert argued that having many small requests is efficient, while large requests are valuable and should not be stalled by waiting for smaller buffers to become available. He also pointed out that the proposed design would add kernel-side complexity due to per-pool wait queue management.

Reviewer Bernd Schubert expressed concerns that the multi-buf-size-per-queue design suboptimally distributes buffers, causing fragmentation and inefficiency in buffer utilization. He argued that separate payload-size queues would allow for better distribution of buffers and more efficient use of available capacity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/14] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to select this option independently of NUMA balancing. The six supported architectures are updated to select this option, and the infrastructure is made available for use beyond NUMA balancing, such as in userfaultfd working set tracking. No functional changes are introduced, but the protnone support is now decoupled from NUMA balancing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/14] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' across various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The change is purely mechanical and does not alter any behavior. The rename reflects that the bit now tracks userfaultfd access rather than just write protection or NUMA hinting. This patch is part of a larger series of changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/14] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal names related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is reused for marking access-tracking PTEs alongside write-protected ones. The rename affects various header files and source code locations across different architectures. The changes are purely mechanical, with no behavior change intended. This is part of a larger patch series that aims to update userfaultfd functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/14] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to enable userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP extends the existing write-protection mechanism to also trap read accesses through userfaultfd. The flag is only meaningful when both PAGE_NONE and uffd PTE bit primitives exist. A Kconfig symbol, CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP, is introduced to auto-select this feature when certain conditions are met. The patch includes changes to various kernel files to add support for the new flag and its associated functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/14] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE, which are used for userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write protection. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP flag installs a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, while the MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE flag restores the original vma->vm_page_prot and clears the uffd bit. These flags are used to ensure that UFFD RWP is handled consistently across different memory types, including anonymous, shared memory, transparent huge pages, and hugetlb. The patch also updates several functions in the mm module to use these new flags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/14] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking could be silently dropped, leading to unexpected behavior when accessing pages with PAGE_NONE semantics. The patch updates several functions to correctly handle the uffd bit and VM_UFFD_RWP flag in these scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/14] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses the handling of VM_UFFD_RWP (User Fault FD Read-Write Protected) in three mm paths: khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. It extends each path to treat VM_UFFD_RWP as VM_UFFD_WP, ensuring that per-PTE RWP state is not silently destroyed or bypassed. The changes involve broadening the VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected(), capping batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP in rmap, and forcing a fault on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT in GUP.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau
review_comments.summary: Responded to questions about the safety of treating VM_UFFD_RWP as VM_UFFD_WP, citing two reasons why it is safe in practice: RWP cannot reach a backing that uses clean-RO + page_mkwrite() dirty-tracking pattern, and for backings that support shared writable + dirty tracking, the fs sets vma->vm_page_prot to RO precisely so the first write traps page_mkwrite().
review_comments.raw_body:
sashiko.dev -- https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260427114607.4068647-1-kas@kernel.org -- wrote:
> > @@ -1084,9 +1092,29 @@ static __always_inline void __copy_present_ptes(struct vm_area_struct *dst_vma,
> >  		pte_t pte, unsigned long addr, int nr)
> >  {
> >  	struct mm_struct *src_mm = src_vma->vm_mm;
> > +	bool writable;
> > +
> > +	/*
> > +	 * Snapshot writability before the RWP-disarm rewrite below: when the
> > +	 * child is not RWP-armed, pte_modify(pte, dst_vma->vm_page_prot) can
> > +	 * silently drop _PAGE_RW from a resolved (no-marker) writable PTE,
> > +	 * so a later pte_write(pte) check would skip the COW wrprotect and
> > +	 * leave the parent writable over a folio shared with the child.
> > +	 */
> > +	writable = pte_write(pte);
> > +
> > +	/*
> > +	 * Child is not RWP-armed: restore accessible protection so the
> > +	 * inherited PAGE_NONE does not cost a fault on first read.
> > +	 */
> > +	if (!userfaultfd_protected(dst_vma)) {
> > +		if (userfaultfd_rwp(src_vma))
> > +			pte = pte_modify(pte, dst_vma->vm_page_prot);
> > +		pte = pte_clear_uffd(pte);
> > +	}
> Does this unconditional pte_modify() create invalid clean and writable PTEs
> for shared mappings?
>
> Without checking pte_uffd(pte) first, this blindly modifies every present PTE
> if the source VMA had RWP enabled. For shared writable mappings, vm_page_prot
> includes _PAGE_RW. If a PTE was clean and mapped read-only to intercept the
> first write for filesystem dirty-tracking, pte_modify() forces the write bit
> on while preserving the clean state.

Two reasons this is safe in practice:

1. RWP cannot reach a backing that uses the clean-RO + page_mkwrite()
   dirty-tracking pattern. vma_can_userfault() admits RWP only on anon,
   shmem and hugetlbfs (the ones with vm_uffd_ops); regular file-backed
   shared mappings are rejected at register time.

2. For backings that *do* support shared writable + dirty tracking (i.e.
   the ones RWP isn't enabled on), the fs sets vma->vm_page_prot to RO
   precisely so the first write traps page_mkwrite(). pte_modify(pte,
   dst_vma->vm_page_prot) would not add _PAGE_RW in that configuration
   either.

> Hardware writes could then bypass page_mkwrite(), leaving the filesystem
> unaware that the page is dirty, and potentially causing silent data loss
> upon reclaim.
> Could this also overwrite other important PTE states like NUMA hinting
> or soft-dirty tracking?

pte_modify() on the supported architectures preserves _PAGE_CHG_MASK,
which covers _PAGE_DIRTY, _PAGE_ACCESSED, _PAGE_SOFT_DIRTY, and the PFN.
NUMA hinting is encoded in the protection bits, so a transient hint
state on a resolved (no-marker) PTE would be lost across this rewrite,
but that's just a re-prime on the next NUMA scan, not a correctness
issue.

> @@ -298,6 +298,16 @@ static __always_inline void change_present_ptes(struct mmu_gather *tlb,
>      else if (uffd_wp_resolve || uffd_rwp_resolve)
>          ptent = pte_clear_uffd(ptent);
>
> +    /*
> +     * The uffd bit on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA carries PROT_NONE
> +     * semantics. If mprotect() or NUMA hinting changed the
> +     * base protection, restore PAGE_NONE so the PTE still
> +     * traps on any access. pte_modify() preserves
> +     * _PAGE_UFFD.
> +     */
> +    if (userfaultfd_rwp(vma) && pte_uffd(ptent))
> +        ptent = pte_modify(ptent, PAGE_NONE);
>
> For VMAs registered with both UFFD WP and RWP, could this silently upgrade
> Write-Protected pages to Read-Write-Protected?

VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP are mutually exclusive per VMA. The combination
is rejected at registration time:

  fs/userfaultfd.c, userfaultfd_register():
        if ((vm_flags & VM_UFFD_WP) && (vm_flags & VM_UFFD_RWP))
                goto out;       /* -EINVAL */

and re-registration cannot drop either bit (also -EBUSY in the same
function), so a PTE in a WP-armed VMA can never satisfy
userfaultfd_rwp(vma); the new branch never fires there.

[ ... ]
> +        /* Re-arm RWP on the moved PTE if dst_vma is RWP-registered. */
> +        if (userfaultfd_rwp(dst_vma)) {
> +            orig_dst_pte = pte_modify(orig_dst_pte, PAGE_NONE);
> +            orig_dst_pte = pte_mkuffd(orig_dst_pte);
> +        }
> +
>          set_pte_at(mm, dst_addr, dst_pte, orig_dst_pte);
>
> Could applying PAGE_NONE unconditionally to newly moved pages trap
> applications in an infinite fault loop?

No -- the post-MOVE access is delivered as a normal RWP fault. In sync
mode it goes to the registered handler, which resolves it with
UFFDIO_RWPROTECT clearing MODE_RWP; in async mode the kernel resolves
it in-kernel and the faulting thread continues. There is no loop.

The semantics here are intentional: a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA has the contract
that every present PTE is either an active marker or a tracked-and-
resolved PTE whose next access will re-trap. UFFDIO_MOVE into such a
VMA must keep that contract, otherwise the moved-in page would be a
silent hole in the working-set view. UFFDIO_MOVE has no mode flag for
"skip protection", by design -- the same way it has no flag to skip
WP arming if dst_vma were WP-armed (and the equivalent could be added
there if we ever decide UFFDIO_MOVE should preserve markers in WP
VMAs too).

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

> > > +		if (userfaultfd_rwp(src_vma))
> > > +			pte = pte_modify(pte, dst_vma->vm_page_prot);
> > > +		pte = pte_clear_uffd(pte);
> > > +	}
> > Does this unconditional pte_modify() create invalid clean and writable PTEs
> > for shared mappings?
> >
> > Without checking pte_uffd(pte) first, this blindly modifies every present PTE
> > if the source VMA had RWP enabled. For shared writable mappings, vm_page_prot
> > includes _PAGE_RW. If a PTE was clean and mapped read-only to intercept the
> > first write for filesystem dirty-tracking, pte_modify() forces the write bit
> > on while preserving the clean state.
> 
> Two reasons this is safe in practice:
> 
> 1. RWP cannot reach a backing that uses the clean-RO + page_mkwrite()
>    dirty-tracking pattern. vma_can_userfault() admits RWP only on anon,
>    shmem and hugetlbfs (the ones with vm_uffd_ops); regular file-backed
>    shared mappings are rejected at register time.
> 
> 2. For backings that *do* support shared writable + dirty tracking (i.e.
>    the ones RWP isn't enabled on), the fs sets vma->vm_page_prot to RO
>    precisely so the first write traps page_mkwrite(). pte_modify(pte,
>    dst_vma->vm_page_prot) would not add _PAGE_RW in that configuration
>    either.
> 
> > Hardware writes could then bypass page_mkwrite(), leaving the filesystem
> > unaware that the page is dirty, and potentially causing silent data loss
> > upon reclaim.
> > Could this also overwrite other important PTE states like NUMA hinting
> > or soft-dirty tracking?
> 
> pte_modify() on the supported architectures preserves _PAGE_CHG_MASK,
> which covers _PAGE_DIRTY, _PAGE_ACCESSED, _PAGE_SOFT_DIRTY, and the PFN.
> NUMA hinting is encoded in the protection bits, so a transient hint
> state on a resolved (no-marker) PTE would be lost across this rewrite,
> but that's just a re-prime on the next NUMA scan, not a correctness
> issue.
> 
> > @@ -298,6 +298,16 @@ static __always_inline void change_present_ptes(struct mmu_gather *tlb,
> >      else if (uffd_wp_resolve || uffd_rwp_resolve)
> >          ptent = pte_clear_uffd(ptent);
> >
> > +    /*
> > +     * The uffd bit on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA carries PROT_NONE
> > +     * semantics. If mprotect() or NUMA hinting changed the
> > +     * base protection, restore PAGE_NONE so the PTE still
> > +     * traps on any access. pte_modify() preserves
> > +     * _PAGE_UFFD.
> > +     */
> > +    if (userfaultfd_rwp(vma) && pte_uffd(ptent))
> > +        ptent = pte_modify(ptent, PAGE_NONE);
> >
> > For VMAs registered with both UFFD WP and RWP, could this silently upgrade
> > Write-Protected pages to Read-Write-Protected?
> 
> VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP are mutually exclusive per VMA. The combination
> is rejected at registration time:
> 
>   fs/userfaultfd.c, userfaultfd_register():
>         if ((vm_flags & VM_UFFD_WP) && (vm_flags & VM_UFFD_RWP))
>                 goto out;       /* -EINVAL */
> 
> and re-registration cannot drop either bit (also -EBUSY in the same
> function), so a PTE in a WP-armed VMA can never satisfy
> userfaultfd_rwp(vma); the new branch never fires there.
> 
> [ ... ]
> > +        /* Re-arm RWP on the moved PTE if dst_vma is RWP-registered. */
> > +        if (userfaultfd_rwp(dst_vma)) {
> > +            orig_dst_pte = pte_modify(orig_dst_pte, PAGE_NONE);
> > +            orig_dst_pte = pte_mkuffd(orig_dst_pte);
> > +        }
> > +
> >          set_pte_at(mm, dst_addr, dst_pte, orig_dst_pte);
> >
> > Could applying PAGE_NONE unconditionally to newly moved pages trap
> > applications in an infinite fault loop?
> 
> No -- the post-MOVE access is delivered as a normal RWP fault. In sync
> mode it goes to the registered handler, which resolves it with
> UFFDIO_RWPROTECT clearing MODE_RWP; in async mode the kernel resolves
> it in-kernel and the faulting thread continues. There is no loop.
> 
> The semantics here are intentional: a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA has the contract
> that every present PTE is either an active marker or a tracked-and-
> resolved PTE whose next access will re-trap. UFFDIO_MOVE into such a
> VMA must keep that contract, otherwise the moved-in page would be a
> silent hole in the working-set view. UFFDIO_MOVE has no mode flag for
> "skip protection", by design -- the same way it has no flag to skip
> WP arming if dst_vma were WP-armed (and the equivalent could be added
> there if we ever decide UFFDIO_MOVE should preserve markers in WP
> VMAs too).
> 

Oopsie. 

I put it in reply to the wrong patch. It suppose to be for 06/14.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds read-write protection (RWP) tracking to the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track and manage RWP on a per-range basis. The patch introduces new ioctls for registering and unregistering RWP ranges, as well as new capability bits and flags. It also updates various kernel functions to handle RWP correctly.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau
review_comments.summary: Confirmed a bug in the RWP registration logic, where it does not require VM_WRITE. Suggested adding a flag to handle this case correctly.
review_comments.raw_body:
sashiko.dev -- https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260427114607.4068647-1-kas@kernel.org -- wrote:
> +    if (enable_rwp)
> +        mm_cp_flags = MM_CP_UFFD_RWP;
> +    else
> +        mm_cp_flags = MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE | MM_CP_TRY_CHANGE_WRITABLE;
>
> Does this unconditionally apply MM_CP_TRY_CHANGE_WRITABLE to the entire range?

Confirmed bug. RWP registration does not require VM_WRITE, so

  mmap(PROT_READ)
  UFFDIO_REGISTER(MODE_RWP)
  UFFDIO_RWPROTECT(<range>, disable)

trips WARN_ON_ONCE(!(vma->vm_flags & VM_WRITE)) inside
maybe_change_pte_writable() on resolve.

The flag belongs inside the iteration, gated on
vma_wants_manual_pte_write_upgrade(vma), matching mwriteprotect_range()
(mm/userfaultfd.c:1003) and userfaultfd_clear_vma() (mm/userfaultfd.c:2182):

        for_each_vma_range(vmi, dst_vma, end) {
                unsigned long vma_start = max(dst_vma->vm_start, start);
                unsigned long vma_end = min(dst_vma->vm_end, end);
                unsigned int flags = mm_cp_flags;

                if (!enable_rwp && vma_wants_manual_pte_write_upgrade(dst_vma))
                        flags |= MM_CP_TRY_CHANGE_WRITABLE;

                change_protection(&tlb, dst_vma, vma_start, vma_end, flags);
        }

Will fold for v2.

> Since change_protection() walks and modifies page tables here, does this
> need to call vma_start_write(vma) first?

No.

This is the same locking pattern as the pre-existing uffd_wp_range() call
that the hunk replaces -- mmap_write_lock without vma_start_write(), which
remains safe for the same reasons:

  - mmap_write_lock excludes anything taking mmap_read_lock, including
    MADV_DONTNEED and the other PTE-page-freeing paths (try_to_free_pte()
    runs under mmap_read_lock + per-VMA locking, never standalone).

  - The remaining concurrent reader is the per-VMA-locked page fault
    (lock_vma_under_rcu()), which walks PTEs under the PTE lock.
    change_protection() also takes the PTE lock when updating, so the
    two serialise. A fault that observes a transient pre-resolve PTE
    just produces a normal fault delivery and resolves correctly.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/14] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for read-write protection (RWP) to the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track and manage RWP-protected pages. The implementation involves modifying various kernel components, including memory management, huge page handling, and userfaultfd code. The goal is to enable efficient tracking of RWP-protected pages and provide a robust mechanism for managing these pages.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The patch author provided a detailed explanation of the RWP fault delivery mechanism and its implementation in various kernel components. They also clarified that the PROT_NONE restoration on swap-in is performed earlier in the series, addressing concerns about swapped-in RWP pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/14] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category, PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, to the pagemap API for tracking read-write protection (RWP) using userfaultfd. It introduces a separate PTE bit for RWP and scopes it to the VMA that registered the mode making the bit meaningful. The patch also updates documentation and code to reflect this change, ensuring that the UAPI does not conflate write-protected and RWP-protected pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/14] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write-protected (RWP) faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines, where the kernel can restore access to pages without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this patch upgrades writable private anonymous PTEs when necessary, preventing COW-faults on re-access of RWP'd writable pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This feature is useful for VMMs that need to switch between sync and async modes on-the-fly, such as during working set scanning or page eviction. The patch also includes changes to ensure that fault-path readers of ctx->features are excluded from seeing intermediate states during the toggle operation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/14] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous RWP modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork handling. The tests verify that UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT work correctly in different situations. The patch includes 11 test cases that run against anonymous, shared memory, and hugetlb memory types.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 14/14] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical workflow for virtual machine (VMM) working-set-tracking, from detection to eviction and back to asynchronous mode.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory using userfaultfd (UFFD). It introduces a new registration mode, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, which allows a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to slower storage. The kernel uses a combination of PTE bits and uffd PTE bit to mark pages as 'userfaultfd-tracked' and keep them resident while inaccessible. In sync mode, the kernel delivers a UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP message to the registered handler, while in async mode, the fault is auto-resolved in-place. Userspace can then learn which pages were touched by reading PAGE_IS_ACCESSED out of PAGEMAP_SCAN. The series also includes preparatory patches to decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING and rename _PAGE_BIT_UFFD_WP-related functions.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the dependency of protnone PTE infrastructure on NUMA balancing by introducing a new config option, ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, which decouples it from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING. The six architectures that support protnone PTEs now select this option and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it.
review_comments.raw_body:
pte_protnone() and pmd_protnone() detect present-but-inaccessible page
table entries. This capability is useful beyond NUMA balancing \u2014 for
example, userfaultfd working set tracking uses protnone PTEs to track
page access without unmapping pages.

Introduce CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE to decouple the protnone PTE
infrastructure from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING. The six architectures that
support protnone PTEs (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch)
now select this option, and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it.

No functional change \u2014 the same set of architectures continues to have
working protnone support, but the infrastructure is now available
independently of NUMA balancing.

review_comments.raw_body:
Preparatory patch for userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP
extends userfaultfd protection from plain write-protection (WP) to
full read-write protection: accesses to an RWP-protected range --
reads as well as writes -- trap through userfaultfd.

RWP marks ranges by combining PAGE_NONE with the uffd PTE bit, so
the flag is only meaningful when both primitives exist. A new
CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP Kconfig symbol auto-selects when CONFIG_64BIT,
CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, and CONFIG_HAVE_ARCH_USERFAULTFD_WP
are all set; call sites that gate on the flag depend on the symbol.
Elsewhere VM_UFFD_RWP aliases VM_NONE and every downstream check
folds to dead code.

Nothing sets the flag yet.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the change_protection() primitive used by userfaultfd RWP, explaining that an RWP-protected PTE is PAGE_NONE with the uffd PTE bit set to distinguish it from a plain mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting fault. The author added two new change_protection() flags: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE, which are wired through various functions so that anon, shmem, THP, and hugetlb all share the same semantics.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern that the uffd PTE bit must survive any kernel path that rewrites a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA's PTE, otherwise the marker carrying PAGE_NONE semantics would be silently dropped and subsequent accesses could leak past RWP tracking. The author has identified several paths where this preservation needs to occur: swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, mprotect(), and userfaultfd.c, and will add necessary code to ensure the uffd bit is preserved in these cases.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the uffd PTE bit is silently destroyed or bypassed in three mm paths: khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. The author agreed to extend each path to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same as VM_UFFD_WP, ensuring per-PTE RWP state is preserved.
review_comments.raw_body:
Three mm paths outside the fault handler gate on the uffd PTE bit
today: khugepaged (skip collapse on ranges carrying markers), rmap
(cap unmap batching), and GUP (force a fault through
gup_can_follow_protnone). Extend each to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same
as VM_UFFD_WP; otherwise per-PTE RWP state is silently destroyed or
bypassed.

khugepaged: try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() and
file_backed_vma_is_retractable() already refuse to collapse or
retract page tables on ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit. Broaden the
VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected() so
VM_UFFD_RWP ranges get the same protection. hpage_collapse_scan_pmd()
needs no change \u2014 its existing pte_uffd() check already catches an
RWP PTE because it carries the uffd bit.

rmap: folio_unmap_pte_batch() caps batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP so
the restore path handles each PTE with its own marker.

GUP: gup_can_follow_protnone() forces a fault on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs
regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. RWP uses protnone as an
access-tracking marker, not for NUMA hinting, so any GUP \u2014 read or
write \u2014 must go through the userfaultfd fault path.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

  * Indicates whether GUP can follow a PROT_NONE mapped page, or whether
- * a (NUMA hinting) fault is required.
+ * a (NUMA hinting or userfaultfd RWP) fault is required.
  */
 static inline bool gup_can_follow_protnone(const struct vm_area_struct *vma,
 					   unsigned int flags)
 {
+	/*
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker, not for
+	 * NUMA hinting. GUP must always take a fault so the access is
+	 * delivered to userfaultfd, regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT.
+	 */
+	if (vma->vm_flags & VM_UFFD_RWP)
+		return false;
+
 	/*
 	 * If callers don't want to honor NUMA hinting faults, no need to
 	 * determine if we would actually have to trigger a NUMA hinting fault.

 		return SCAN_VMA_CHECK;
 
-	/* Keep pmd pgtable for uffd-wp; see comment in retract_page_tables() */
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	/*
+	 * Keep pmd pgtable while the uffd bit is in use; see comment in
+	 * retract_page_tables().
+	 */
+	if (userfaultfd_protected(vma))
 		return SCAN_PTE_UFFD;
 
 	folio = filemap_lock_folio(vma->vm_file->f_mapping,
@@ -1746,13 +1749,14 @@ static bool file_backed_vma_is_retractable(struct vm_area_struct *vma)
 		return false;

-	 * When a vma is registered with uffd-wp, we cannot recycle
+	 * When a vma is registered with uffd-wp or RWP, we cannot recycle
 	 * the page table because there may be pte markers installed.
-	 * Other vmas can still have the same file mapped hugely, but
-	 * skip this one: it will always be mapped in small page size
-	 * for uffd-wp registered ranges.
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP ranges similarly rely on per-PTE uffd state
+	 * and cannot be recycled to a shared PMD. Other vmas can still
+	 * have the same file mapped hugely, but skip this one: it will
+	 * always be mapped in small page size for these registrations.
 	 */
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	if (userfaultfd_protected(vma))
 		return false;
 
 	/*

index 05056c213203..1426d1ece917 100644
--- a/mm/rmap.c
+++ b/mm/rmap.c
@@ -1965,7 +1965,7 @@ static inline unsigned int folio_unmap_pte_batch(struct folio *folio,

-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))

review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about re-registering a range dropping per-PTE markers, explained that the current implementation rejects such combinations and requires an unregister first to avoid stale markers. The author confirmed this behavior is intentional and consistent with older kernels.

review_comments.author: Kiryl (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed concerns about the handling of read-write protected (RWP) pages in userfaultfd, specifically that the code was not correctly waiting for userspace to resolve RWP faults. The author rewired the fault side of RWP tracking and added matching waiters for sync-mode fault handlers.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed concerns about the safety of using RWP on certain backing types by explaining that it is safe due to two reasons: (1) RWP cannot reach backings that use clean-RO + page_mkwrite() dirty-tracking pattern, and (2) for backings that support shared writable + dirty tracking, the fs sets vma->vm_page_prot to RO, preventing _PAGE_RW from being added.

The author addressed concerns about preserving _PAGE_CHG_MASK in pte_modify() and its impact on NUMA hinting, explaining that it's not a correctness issue but rather a transient state that will be re-evaluated on the next NUMA scan.

The author addressed concerns about VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP being mutually exclusive, explaining that they are rejected at registration time due to -EINVAL or -EBUSY errors, making it impossible for a PTE in a WP-armed VMA to satisfy userfaultfd_rwp(vma).

The author addressed a concern about the behavior of UFFDIO_MOVE into a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA, explaining that it must keep the contract of having every present PTE either as an active marker or a tracked-and-resolved PTE. The author confirmed this is intentional and by design.
review_comments.raw_body:
Two reasons this is safe in practice:

1. RWP cannot reach a backing that uses the clean-RO + page_mkwrite()
   dirty-tracking pattern. vma_can_userfault() admits RWP only on anon,
   shmem and hugetlbfs (the ones with vm_uffd_ops); regular file-backed
   shared mappings are rejected at register time.

2. For backings that *do* support shared writable + dirty tracking (i.e.
   the ones RWP isn't enabled on), the fs sets vma->vm_page_prot to RO
   precisely so the first write traps page_mkwrite(). pte_modify(pte,
   dst_vma->vm_page_prot) would not add _PAGE_RW in that configuration
   either.

---

pte_modify() on the supported architectures preserves _PAGE_CHG_MASK,
which covers _PAGE_DIRTY, _PAGE_ACCESSED, _PAGE_SOFT_DIRTY, and the PFN.
NUMA hinting is encoded in the protection bits, so a transient hint
state on a resolved (no-marker) PTE would be lost across this rewrite,
but that's just a re-prime on the next NUMA scan, not a correctness
issue.

---

VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP are mutually exclusive per VMA. The combination
is rejected at registration time:

  fs/userfaultfd.c, userfaultfd_register():
        if ((vm_flags & VM_UFFD_WP) && (vm_flags & VM_UFFD_RWP))
                goto out;       /* -EINVAL */

and re-registration cannot drop either bit (also -EBUSY in the same
function), so a PTE in a WP-armed VMA can never satisfy
userfaultfd_rwp(vma); the new branch never fires there.

[ ... ]

---

No -- the post-MOVE access is delivered as a normal RWP fault. In sync
mode it goes to the registered handler, which resolves it with
UFFDIO_RWPROTECT clearing MODE_RWP; in async mode the kernel resolves
it in-kernel and the faulting thread continues. There is no loop.

The semantics here are intentional: a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA has the contract
that every present PTE is either an active marker or a tracked-and-
resolved PTE whose next access will re-trap. UFFDIO_MOVE into such a
VMA must keep that contract, otherwise the moved-in page would be a
silent hole in the working-set view. UFFDIO_MOVE has no mode flag for
"skip protection", by design -- the same way it has no flag to skip
WP arming if dst_vma were WP-armed (and the equivalent could be added
there if we ever decide UFFDIO_MOVE should preserve markers in WP
VMAs too).

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a bug where RWP registration fails due to missing VM_WRITE flag in the mmap call, and agreed to fix it by moving the flag inside the iteration in v2.

The author responded to concerns about the locking pattern in uffd_range(), explaining that it is safe because mmap_write_lock excludes concurrent readers, including MADV_DONTNEED and other PTE-page-freeing paths, and serializes with per-VMA-locked page faults.
review_comments.raw_body:
Confirmed bug. RWP registration does not require VM_WRITE, so

  mmap(PROT_READ)
  UFFDIO_REGISTER(MODE_RWP)
  UFFDIO_RWPROTECT(<range>, disable)

trips WARN_ON_ONCE(!(vma->vm_flags & VM_WRITE)) inside
maybe_change_pte_writable() on resolve.

The flag belongs inside the iteration, gated on
vma_wants_manual_pte_write_upgrade(vma), matching mwriteprotect_range()
(mm/userfaultfd.c:1003) and userfaultfd_clear_vma() (mm/userfaultfd.c:2182):

        for_each_vma_range(vmi, dst_vma, end) {
                unsigned long vma_start = max(dst_vma->vm_start, start);
                unsigned long vma_end = min(dst_vma->vm_end, end);
                unsigned int flags = mm_cp_flags;

                if (!enable_rwp && vma_wants_manual_pte_write_upgrade(dst_vma))
                        flags |= MM_CP_TRY_CHANGE_WRITABLE;

                change_protection(&tlb, dst_vma, vma_start, vma_end, flags);
        }

Will fold for v2.

---

No.

This is the same locking pattern as the pre-existing uffd_wp_range() call
that the hunk replaces -- mmap_write_lock without vma_start_write(), which
remains safe for the same reasons:

  - mmap_write_lock excludes anything taking mmap_read_lock, including
    MADV_DONTNEED and the other PTE-page-freeing paths (try_to_free_pte()
    runs under mmap_read_lock + per-VMA locking, never standalone).

  - The remaining concurrent reader is the per-VMA-locked page fault
    (lock_vma_under_rcu()), which walks PTEs under the PTE lock.
    change_protection() also takes the PTE lock when updating, so the
    two serialise. A fault that observes a transient pre-resolve PTE
    just produces a normal fault delivery and resolves correctly.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about PROT_NONE restoration on swap-in, explaining that it was already handled in patch 6/14 by modifying the do_swap_page() function to set the PTE to PAGE_NONE | _PAGE_UFFD instead of PROT_READ | _PAGE_UFFD. No fix is planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
The PROT_NONE restoration on swap-in is performed two commits earlier,
in patch 6/14 "mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites", which
adds to do_swap_page():

        if (pte_swp_uffd(vmf->orig_pte) && userfaultfd_rwp(vma))
                pte = pte_modify(pte, PAGE_NONE);

so a swapped-in RWP page comes back as PAGE_NONE | _PAGE_UFFD, not
PROT_READ | _PAGE_UFFD. The same patch covers unuse_pte() (the
swapoff(2) path), restore_exclusive_pte(), and the migration-entry
resolvers; each gates on userfaultfd_rwp(vma) and the swap-pte uffd
bit before re-applying PAGE_NONE.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 01/45] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 01/45] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 02/45] mm: page_alloc: per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator to improve page allocation performance on large machines. It addresses two common patterns of contention: affinity violations and concurrent exits, by extending the per-cpu (PCP) allocator to operate on whole pageblocks with ownership tracking. The PCP now claims pageblocks from the zone buddy, splits them locally, and tags pages with their owning CPU, eliminating affinity violations and reducing lock contention. The patch also introduces a four-phase refill approach for the PCP, which includes recovering owned fragments, claiming whole pageblocks, grabbing sub-pageblock chunks, and traditional __rmqueue() with migratetype fallback. This allows for more efficient allocation and merging of pages, reducing fragmentation and improving overall performance.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 03/45] mm: page_alloc: use trylock for PCP lock in free path to avoid lock inversion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a deadlock scenario in the Linux kernel's page allocation code by always using trylock for the PCP lock in the free path, rather than blocking on it. This is necessary because the allocation path acquires the PCP lock with interrupts enabled, while other paths may hold locks that are also taken in hardirq context, creating a potential ABBA deadlock. The patch restores the mainline invariant of never blocking on PCP lock acquisition in the free path by using spin_trylock_irqsave() and falling back to the zone buddy when the trylock fails.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 04/45] mm: mm_init: fix zone assignment for pages in unavailable ranges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem in the Linux kernel where pages in memory holes between memblock regions were being assigned to the wrong zone, leading to potential accounting confusion or crashes. The fix involves looking up the correct zone for each page in the hole during initialization, which has no performance impact since it only runs once at boot time.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 05/45] mm: vmstat: restore per-migratetype free counts in /proc/pagetypeinfo
series_items.patch_summary: This patch restores the display of per-migratetype free counts in /proc/pagetypeinfo, which was broken by the SPB rework. The counts are accumulated from the per-superpageblock free lists and then emitted as one line per migratetype. To avoid time under the lock, the zone->lock is dropped between superpageblocks, allowing for concurrent memory hotplug to swap zone->superpageblocks during a lock drop. This patch is intended for a debug-only interface and does not guarantee consistent counts in such scenarios.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 06/45] mm: page_alloc: remove watermark boost mechanism
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the watermark boost mechanism, a feature introduced to react to fragmentation events at the pageblock granularity. The mechanism was found to be ineffective in superpageblock environments and caused unnecessary wakeups of kswapd even when free memory was abundant. The patch drops several related functions and variables, including boost_watermark(), ZONE_BOOSTED_WATERMARK flag, and watermark_boost_factor sysctl. This change aims to simplify the page allocation code and reduce unnecessary workloads on the system.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 07/45] mm: page_alloc: async evacuation of stolen movable pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a lightweight asynchronous evacuation mechanism to prevent memory fragmentation caused by movable pageblocks being stolen for unmovable or reclaimable allocations. When a movable pageblock is claimed, it queues a work item to migrate the remaining movable pages out, allowing future unmovable/reclaimable allocations to be satisfied from the now-evacuated block. This approach reduces long-term memory fragmentation and keeps allocation types concentrated.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 08/45] mm: page_alloc: track actual page contents in pageblock flags
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the pageblock_data flags to track the actual types of pages allocated within a pageblock, independent of its intended migratetype. The new flags PB_has_unmovable, PB_has_reclaimable, and PB_has_movable are set at allocation time and cleared when the pageblock is full or migration confirms no pages of that type remain. This allows for superpageblock-level steering decisions to direct future allocations to already-tainted regions, keeping clean regions available for large contiguous allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 09/45] mm: page_alloc: introduce superpageblock metadata for 1GB anti-fragmentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new data structure called superpageblock to track pageblock composition at a coarser granularity, specifically targeting 1GB anti-fragmentation. The superpageblock groups pageblocks and maintains counts of pageblocks by migratetype, as well as a list_head for future organization by fullness category. This is done to enable steering of unmovable/reclaimable allocations into already-tainted superpageblocks and preserve clean superpageblocks for 1GB hugepage allocation. The patch adds new fields to the mmzone.h header file and modifies several other files to implement this new data structure, with no changes to allocation behavior.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 10/45] mm: page_alloc: support superpageblock resize for memory hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for resizing superpageblocks when memory is hotplugged. It introduces a new function, resize_zone_superpageblocks(), which allocates a new superpageblock array covering the full zone span and copies existing superpageblocks. This ensures that pages in newly added regions participate in anti-fragmentation steering. The patch also adds a flag to track whether superpageblocks were allocated using kvmalloc for hotplug memory. ZONE_DEVICE is excluded from this feature as device pages should not be subject to anti-fragmentation steering.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 11/45] mm: page_alloc: add superpageblock fullness lists for allocation steering
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new data structure to track the fullness of superpageblocks, which are groups of pageblocks that can be allocated together as a single unit. The fullness is tracked in five buckets (FULL, 75%, 50%, 25%, ALMOST_EMPTY) and two categories (CLEAN vs TAINTED), allowing for efficient allocation steering without sorting. A separate list tracks completely free superpageblocks. Additionally, the patch adds a debugfs interface to display information about superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 12/45] mm: page_alloc: steer pageblock stealing to tainted superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to prefer pages from tainted superpageblocks when stealing movable pageblocks for unmovable or reclaimable allocations. This is done by performing a bounded scan of the free list to find a tainted alternative if the original page belongs to a clean superpageblock. The goal is to concentrate contamination in already impure superpageblocks, preserving clean ones for future 1GB hugepage allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 13/45] mm: page_alloc: steer movable allocations to fullest clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch steers movable allocations to fullest clean superpageblocks in the Linux kernel's memory management system. The goal is to pack movable allocations into already-partial superpageblocks, preserving empty superpageblocks for potential huge page allocation. To achieve this, a new function `sb_preferred_for_movable` is added to find the fullest clean superpageblock, and another function `__rmqueue_from_sb` is introduced to scan the free list within a specific superpageblock's PFN range. The patch hooks into the existing `rmqueue_bulk` phase 1 to try the preferred superpageblock before falling back to the normal allocation path.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 14/45] mm: page_alloc: extract claim_whole_block from try_to_claim_block
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic of claiming a whole pageblock in the Linux kernel's memory management system. It extracts this logic into a standalone function called `claim_whole_block`, which handles the transition from free to used, changes the migratetype of the pageblock, and splits the block down to the requested order. This is done as part of a larger effort to prepare for reusing this logic in future patches related to per-superpageblock free lists. The patch makes no functional changes and is considered a pure refactoring effort.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 15/45] mm: page_alloc: add per-superpageblock free lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-superpageblock free lists to the Linux kernel's page allocation system, enabling precise allocation steering by selecting the right superpageblock first and then allocating from its local free list in O(1). The patch introduces a new data structure for tracking free pages within each superpageblock, allowing for more efficient allocation of large memory blocks. It also updates various functions to use the per-superpageblock free lists, including __rmqueue_smallest, __rmqueue_claim, and __rmqueue_steal. This change aims to improve memory allocation performance by reducing the number of searches required to find available memory blocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 16/45] mm: page_alloc: add background superpageblock defragmentation worker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a background worker to defragment superpageblocks when free space is low. The worker evacuates movable pages from tainted superpageblocks, targeting specific blocks rather than scanning the entire system. Defrag is triggered when a tainted block has less than 2 pageblocks of free space or no movable pages remain. Clean superpageblocks are skipped as they don't need defragmentation. The patch replaces an older per-pageblock evacuation plumbing with a more efficient background worker.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 17/45] mm: page_alloc: add within-superpageblock compaction for clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the superpageblock defragmentation framework to handle clean superpageblocks in addition to tainted ones. It introduces a new compaction logic that migrates pages within the same superpageblock to consolidate scattered free pages into whole free pageblocks. The key components include a dispatcher function, a custom migration target allocator, and modifications to existing functions to make them superpageblock-aware. This patch aims to improve memory defragmentation efficiency by reducing fragmentation within clean superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 18/45] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock-aware contiguous and higher order allocation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces superpageblock-aware contiguous page allocation, which leverages SPB metadata to find clean (all-free) superpageblocks and allocate large contiguous pages more efficiently. It adds a new field to the superpageblock structure to track fully-covered allocations and updates the iteration logic to handle non-aligned allocation boundaries. The patch also hooks this new allocation method into the existing compaction code for THP/mTHP and high-order unmovable/reclaimable allocations, and tries sb_try_alloc_contig() first for movable allocations at pageblock_order or above.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 19/45] mm: page_alloc: prevent atomic allocations from tainting clean SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents atomic allocations from tainting clean superpage blocks (SPBs) by introducing two changes to the page allocation process. First, it extends the Pass 2 of __rmqueue_smallest to search for sub-pageblock-order free blocks in tainted SPBs and attempts to claim them using try_to_claim_block. Second, it introduces an intermediate flag ALLOC_NOFRAG_TAINTED_OK that allows atomic allocations to steal from tainted SPBs before dropping protection entirely. This approach aims to prevent the primary source of taint spreading observed on production systems.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 20/45] mm: page_alloc: aggressively pack non-movable allocations in tainted SPBs on large systems
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new threshold, SPB_AGGRESSIVE_THRESHOLD, to control when the kernel aggressively packs non-movable allocations into tainted superpageblocks (SPBs) on large systems. On systems with more than 8 superpageblocks, it relaxes the minimum order floor for tainted SPBs, allowing non-movable allocations to consume free space at any granularity. This is done to prevent unnecessary tainting of clean SPBs and preserve MOVABLE capacity within tainted SPBs. The patch preserves the original behavior on small systems with fewer than 8 superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 21/45] mm: page_alloc: prefer reclaim over tainting clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch restructures the Linux kernel's memory allocation fallback cascade to prefer reclaim and compaction over tainting clean superpageblocks (SPBs). When allocating pages, it first tries to reclaim free pages from existing tainted SPBs before considering clean SPBs. This change helps maintain the homogeneity of clean SPBs by reducing the number of tainted SPBs created during heavy workloads. The patch also updates the slowpath allocation logic to preserve the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag and drop it only as a last resort after reclaim and compaction have failed.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 22/45] mm: page_alloc: adopt partial pageblocks from tainted superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new phase to the rmqueue_bulk function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code. The new phase, Phase 2, searches tainted superpageblocks for partially-free pageblocks that match the requested migratetype and claims ownership of them. This reduces fragmentation spread by concentrating non-movable allocations into already-tainted superpageblocks. The patch also includes a check to prevent two CPUs from claiming ownership of the same pageblock simultaneously.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 23/45] mm: page_alloc: add CONFIG_DEBUG_VM sanity checks for SPB counters
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds sanity checks to the page allocation code in the Linux kernel to ensure that per-superpageblock counters (nr_free, nr_unmovable, nr_reclaimable, and nr_movable) do not exceed the total number of pageblocks. The checks are only enabled when CONFIG_DEBUG_VM is set, which allows for early detection of counter drift during stress testing without affecting production performance. The patch includes a new function spb_debug_check() that performs these checks and is called after each operation that mutates these counters. Several real bugs in the SPB stack were caught by this check during development, making it a valuable addition to the kernel code.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 24/45] mm: page_alloc: targeted evacuation and dynamic reserves for tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces the proliferation of tainted superpageblocks by introducing two changes to the page allocation algorithm. Firstly, it dynamically scales the movable steering reserve based on the size of the superpageblock, ensuring that there is always a sufficient amount of free space for unmovable and reclaimable claims. Secondly, it implements a two-phase targeted evacuation process before dropping ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT allocations, which evacuates movable pages from tainted superpageblocks to create free pageblocks and avoid tainting clean superpageblocks unnecessarily.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 25/45] mm: page_alloc: skip pageblock compatibility threshold in tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory allocation system to skip a compatibility threshold when trying to relabel a pageblock as non-movable within a tainted superpage block (SPB). The threshold, which is currently enforced by try_to_claim_block(), checks if at least half of the pages in the pageblock are free. However, this check is unnecessary and even harmful within a tainted SPB, where fragmentation has already been accepted. By skipping this check, the patch aims to reduce the number of failed relabeling attempts and prevent the tainting of additional clean SPBs. The change is expected to improve memory allocation performance under heavy load conditions.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 26/45] mm: page_alloc: prevent UNMOVABLE/RECLAIMABLE mixing in pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the mixing of UNMOVABLE and RECLAIMABLE page allocations within a single pageblock, which can lead to internal fragmentation and wasted memory. The issue arises when free pages of different types are not distinguishable by the buddy allocator's compatibility heuristic. To address this, the patch introduces a new helper function to detect cross-type pairs and tightens two paths in the page allocator: try_to_claim_block() and __rmqueue_steal(). The changes ensure that UNMOVABLE and RECLAIMABLE pages are not mixed within a pageblock, allowing for more efficient memory usage. The test plan involves building and rebooting on a bare-metal devvm with existing tainted SPBs to verify the improvements in free distribution across different order sizes.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 27/45] mm: trigger deferred SPB evacuation when atomic allocs would taint a clean SPB
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory allocation system to trigger deferred superpage block (SPB) evacuation when an atomic allocation would taint a clean SPB. The patch introduces new data structures and functions to manage deferred SPB evacuations, which are scheduled on the zone's workqueue. This approach aims to reduce the number of clean SPBs being tainted by atomic allocations, allowing subsequent allocations to claim pages from tainted pools instead. The trigger is gated by prechecks to prevent storming the workqueue.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 28/45] mm: page_alloc: keep PCP refill in tainted SPBs across owned pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the rmqueue_bulk function in the Linux kernel's mm/page_alloc.c file to improve PCP refill performance on systems with many CPUs and tainted superpageblocks. The original code abandoned candidate pageblocks if they were already owned by another CPU, but this patch takes pages from owned pageblocks without claiming ownership or marking them as PagePCPBuddy. This change allows the atomic alloc to succeed more often and reduces the number of clean SPBs that get tainted.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 29/45] mm: page_alloc: refuse fragmenting fallback for callers with cheap fallback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page allocation mechanism to refuse fragmentation fallbacks for certain callers that have a cheap fallback option. It introduces a new flag, ALLOC_HIGHORDER_OPTIONAL, which is set for callers with explicit fallback declarations or atomic-context shapes. The patch also adds a struct to record tainted SPB walks and checks for tainted SPBs in the __rmqueue function. If a tainted SPB has free pages or a free pageblock that could absorb an allocation after evacuation, the patch returns NULL and bumps SPB_HIGHORDER_REFUSED. This prevents fragmentation fallbacks for callers with cheap fallback options, allowing truly must-not-fail consumers to proceed with their allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 30/45] mm: page_alloc: drive slab shrink from SPB anti-fragmentation pressure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new mechanism to drive slab shrink from SPB anti-fragmentation pressure, which prevents the system from running out of memory due to excessive slab growth. The problem it solves is that GFP_KERNEL slab callers have no fallback and can lead to sustained slab growth, draining the tainted pool and causing fragmentation. To address this, the patch adds a parallel slab-shrink mechanism that mirrors the existing evacuation infrastructure, allowing for the consolidation of movable pages out of tainted SPBs and freeing up pageblocks for re-coalescing. The new mechanism is triggered by three sites: __rmqueue_smallest, __rmqueue_claim, and end of spb_evacuate_for_order. This approach aims to prevent the system from plateauing at a high tainted-SPB count and instead maintain equilibrium at a lower level.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 31/45] mm: page_alloc: cross-non-movable buddy borrow within tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where pages freed via __free_one_page are placed on the per-SPB free list determined by their pageblock's migratetype, not the original allocation's migratetype. This leads to structural mismatches in slab-heavy workloads, causing RECLAIMABLE pageblocks to fill up with live slab objects and UNMOVABLE pageblocks to accumulate sparse free space. To fix this, a new Pass 2c is added between Pass 2b and 3: for non-movable allocations that couldn't find their own migratetype, it tries borrowing a sub-pageblock buddy from the opposite non-movable migratetype's free list within tainted SPBs. This allows UNMOV allocs to check RECL free lists and vice versa, reducing the number of tainted SPBs created during RECL bursts.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 32/45] mm: page_alloc: proactive high-water trigger for SPB slab shrink
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a proactive high-water trigger to the SPB slab shrinker, which fires when a tainted SPB's free pages fall below a certain threshold due to non-movable allocations. This allows for more frequent triggering of the shrinker, rather than waiting until the SPB is exhausted. The patch also removes a 100ms time-based throttle from the queue_spb_slab_shrink function, allowing the worker's runtime to act as the natural rate-limiter. The changes aim to improve the responsiveness and effectiveness of the slab shrinker in managing memory pressure.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 33/45] mm: page_alloc: refuse to taint clean SPBs for atomic NORETRY callers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page allocation logic in Linux to prevent clean superpageblocks from being tainted when an atomic allocation request is made without retrying. The patch targets callers that pass __GFP_NORETRY, which indicates they have a fallback path for failure. In such cases, tainting a clean superpageblock would incur a lasting cost and block future allocations. The patch skips the relaxation steps for NORETRY callers and returns NULL immediately to allow their fallback paths to handle the failure.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 34/45] mm: page_reporting: walk per-superpageblock free lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the page reporting cycle to correctly handle systems with superpageblocks. Instead of walking a single zone-level list, it iterates over each superpageblock's free lists and uses a shared budget to prevent fragmentation from multiplying the rate limit. The memory hotplug read lock is held around the SPB walk to ensure thread safety. A fallback path for zones without allocated SPB arrays is retained.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 35/45] mm: show_mem: collect migratetype letters from per-superpageblock lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the show_mem() function to correctly display migratetype letters for each order in a zone. After the superpage block (SPB) rework, free pages are stored on per-superpageblock lists instead of zone-level lists. The patch iterates over each SPB in the zone and checks if any migratetypes have free pages at that order. If all migratetypes have been seen, it exits early. The patch also handles zones whose SPB array has not yet been allocated by falling back to the zone-level free_area. This change ensures that show_mem() provides accurate information about available memory in the system.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 36/45] mm: page_alloc: add alloc_flags parameter to __rmqueue_smallest
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an 'alloc_flags' parameter to the __rmqueue_smallest function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code. The alloc_flags parameter is passed through to subsequent functions, allowing them to observe and react to allocation flags. This change does not introduce any behavioral changes, as the added parameter is currently ignored within __rmqueue_smallest. The patch is part of a larger series (45 patches) and is intended to facilitate future logic that requires knowledge of allocation flags.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 37/45] mm/slub: kvmalloc — add __GFP_NORETRY to large-kmalloc attempt
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the behavior of kvmalloc's large memory allocation attempts by adding a __GFP_NORETRY flag to signal that the caller has a fallback path, such as vmalloc. This change prevents the page allocator from tainting previously clean superpageblocks in an attempt to satisfy the kmalloc request, which can lead to performance issues and wasted resources. The patch addresses a problem where large kvmalloc requests were inadvertently tainting clean superpageblocks, causing subsequent hugepage allocations to fail. The approach taken is to add the __GFP_NORETRY flag in the same conditional that strips __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM, allowing the page allocator to skip unnecessary retries and return NULL immediately when it encounters this flag.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 38/45] mm: page_alloc: per-(zone, order, mt) PASS_1 hint cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a hint cache to improve the performance of page allocation in Linux. The cache stores the most recently used superpage block (SPB) for each zone, order, and migration type tuple. This allows the kernel to quickly retrieve the next SPB to allocate from, reducing the number of list walks required. The patch also includes a mechanism to invalidate stale hints when the SPB array is resized due to memory hotplug additions. Measurements show a significant reduction in median walk depth and tail visits, with a hint hit rate of 99%. The memory cost of the cache is estimated to be around 320 bytes per zone plus 2.6 KB per CPU.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 39/45] mm: debug: prevent infinite recursion in dump_page() with CMA
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents infinite recursion in the dump_page() function when dealing with Contiguous Memory Allocation (CMA). It does this by adding a guard to check if the page frame number is valid and if the zone spans that pfn before calling is_migrate_cma_folio(). This change allows dump_page() to safely report on pages without a meaningful zone, preventing infinite recursion due to VM_BUG_ON_PAGE firing. The patch was found during early boot testing where a page in an unavailable range caused stack exhaustion.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 40/45] PM: hibernate: walk per-superpageblock free lists in mark_free_pages
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the mark_free_pages function in the kernel's power management code to correctly handle free pages after the introduction of superpage blocks (SPBs). The existing function walked the buddy allocator's free lists, but since SPBs were introduced, free pages now live on per-superpageblock free lists. This patch adds a loop to iterate over each superpage block and its corresponding free list, ensuring that all free pages are correctly marked as such during hibernation. If a zone has no superpage blocks (e.g., an unpopulated hotplug zone), the function falls back to using the zone-level free list. This change improves the accuracy of the hibernation snapshot and prevents unnecessary memory usage.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 41/45] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows extent buffer pages allocated by the btrfs file system to be marked as movable, which enables them to be migrated and freed up memory. The change targets a common fragmentation issue where these pages are pinned in place, preventing large allocations from occurring. By adding the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to the allocation of these pages, they can now be moved out of the way, reducing memory pressure and allowing for more efficient use of system resources.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 42/45] mm: page_alloc: cross-MOV borrow within tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new pass, Pass 2d, in the page allocation algorithm to borrow individual buddies from tainted SPBs' movable free list when other passes fail. This approach aims to reduce the number of times a fresh clean SPB needs to be tainted, which can lead to fragmentation and negatively impact anti-fragmentation invariants. The borrowed pages are used for non-movable allocations and returned to the movable list upon freeing, allowing compaction within tainted pageblocks until the allocation is freed.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 43/45] mm: page_alloc: trigger defrag from allocator hot path on tainted-SPB pressure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two triggers to the page allocator's hot path to start defragmentation on tainted sub-page blocks (SPBs) that are under pressure. The first trigger fires when a successful allocation already evaluates the SPB's shrink threshold, and the second trigger walks the tainted-SPB list before fragmenting the clean pool. These triggers aim to catch stuck SPBs with no allocator activity to drive defragmentation, while still respecting the cooldown gate to prevent storming the worker. The patch also centralizes the reaction to tainted allocations into a helper function.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 44/45] mm: page_alloc: SPB tracepoint instrumentation [DROP-FOR-UPSTREAM]
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces instrumentation for the SPB (Split Page) anti-fragmentation mechanism in the Linux kernel's page allocation code. It adds several tracepoints to track various events, such as page allocations, tainting of pages, and transitions between different migration types. The instrumentation is designed for diagnostic purposes only and can be dropped before upstream submission. The patch bundles all SPB-related tracepoint definitions and call sites into a single commit.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 45/45] mm: page_alloc: enlarge and unify spb_evacuate_for_order
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses two issues in the slowpath of __alloc_pages_slowpath: a per-call budget that is too small and a source-pageblock migratetype filter that creates blind spots. The patch introduces new constants to increase the evacuation budget, removes the migratetype filter, and collapses the two-phase structure of spb_evacuate_for_order into a single pass. This allows for more efficient evacuation of movable pageblocks and reduces the likelihood of dropping ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT. The changes are intended to improve performance by allowing the slowpath to take advantage of available movable content in tainted SPBs.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the packed pageblock_flags bitmap in the Linux kernel's memory management code with a per-pageblock struct called pageblock_data, which contains its own flags word. This change simplifies the accessor code and reduces cache misses by colocating migratetype and pageblock ownership lookups in the same struct. The memory overhead of this change is 8 bytes per pageblock, but it is considered negligible. No functional changes are made.
